text: Alright chat, welcome back to the stream. Tonight we are watching something truly special. This is The Sunshine Makers from 1935, an animated short sponsored by Borden Milk, and I need you to understand right now that this is not a normal cartoon. This is a fever dream someone greenlit during the Great Depression, and it is about to get very strange. We are talking milk. Weaponized milk.
label: cold_open

text: So the film opens and we are in this bright, idyllic little village. Everything is orange, everything is warm. The trees are round, the houses are round, even the clouds look like someone baked them. And living in this village are these tiny cheerful gnome creatures who have exactly one job... bottling sunshine. Literal sunshine, into literal glass bottles. This is their entire economy.
label: opening_village

text: The gnomes wake up, they stretch, they put on their little pointy hats, and they march off to the sunshine factory. There is a machine that captures rays of sun and fills up milk bottles. I need you to sit with this image. A factory that bottles sunlight. In 1935 someone drew this frame by frame and thought this was a reasonable premise for selling milk.
label: sunshine_factory

text: The gnomes are dancing while they work because of course they are. They are orange, they are happy, they are probably hopped up on vitamin D. They sing a little song about making sunshine. Meanwhile, across the forest in a dark cave... oh chat... this is where it gets good. There is a whole civilization of sad blue goblins.
label: goblin_reveal

text: These goblins are not just a little sad. They are existentially devastated. They shuffle around hunched over like they just read their own Wikipedia page. Everything in their world is blue and grey and cold. They have no sunshine. They have no milk. They have no joy. And the gnomes look over at them from their bright orange paradise and think... we can fix this.
label: sad_goblins

text: Now a reasonable cartoon at this point would have the gnomes walk over and share their bottled sunshine peacefully. That is not what happens. The gnomes load up catapults. I repeat, they load up actual medieval siege weapons with glass bottles of liquid sunshine, and they start launching them at the depressed goblins. This is projectile dairy warfare and I am still not making any of this up.
label: catapults

text: The bottles go flying through the air, shattering against the goblins, and the goblins... they start drinking it. And chat, the moment that sunshine milk hits their system, they turn orange. Their entire color palette changes in real time. Blue to orange. Sad to happy. Clinical depression to... whatever the opposite of clinical depression is. Clinical dairy-induced euphoria.
label: transformation

text: The goblins start dancing. They cannot control it. Their bodies are moving against their will, forced into happiness by the power of fortified milk. One of them does a little jig that honestly looks like he is having an existential crisis and a dance recital at the same time. The gnomes are watching this from their catapults like, yes, our work here is done.
label: forced_dancing

text: There is one goblin in particular who is being very stubborn about this whole thing. He is hiding behind a rock, arms crossed, refusing to be happy. The gnomes see him. They target him specifically. Bottles are raining down around him like a dairy airstrike, and eventually he has no choice. He drinks. He turns orange. He dances. The resistance is futile. Milk wins.
label: stubborn_goblin

text: Now let me talk about the animation quality here because this is 1935 and it is stunning. This was done in Cinecolor, which was an early two-color process. Everything warm is rendered in these beautiful oranges and golds, everything cold in these deep blues. The contrast is incredible. It's like if Wes Anderson directed a silent film about dairy products.
label: animation_quality

text: The character design is pure rubber hose animation, which if you don't know, was the dominant style before Disney standardized everything. The limbs bend like garden hoses, there are no joints, everyone moves like they are made of noodles. The goblins especially have this wonderful hunched posture, their noses practically dragging on the ground, like they've been carrying the weight of the world.
label: rubber_hose

text: So at this point in the film, the goblin king realizes what is happening and he is furious. He calls a war council. All the remaining blue goblins gather in the cave and the king gives this incredible rousing speech about how they will not be forced to be happy. The speech is completely silent because this is 1935 and dialogue was expensive, but the animation sells it perfectly.
label: goblin_council

text: The goblins prepare for battle. They are gathering weapons, they are fortifying the cave, they are ready to fight for their right to be miserable. And this is where the film takes its most unhinged turn. The ending is approaching and I need you to understand that what happens next changes everything we thought we knew about this cartoon.
label: battle_prep

text: The final confrontation is genuinely epic. The gnomes are on one side with their catapults full of bottled sunshine. The goblins are on the other, grim and determined to defend their sadness. Sunshine bottles are flying. Goblins are ducking. It is complete chaos. And then something happens that I was not prepared for. The gnomes deploy their ultimate weapon.
label: final_battle

text: They wheel out a giant tank. A literal tank filled with sunshine milk. It has a nozzle on the front. They start spraying goblins with industrial quantities of dairy product. Goblins are being knocked over by the force of the spray. They are getting up orange. They are dancing against their will. This is the most intense milk-based combat scene ever committed to animation.
label: milk_tank

text: The goblin king is the last one standing. He is cowering behind his throne, watching his entire kingdom get forcibly converted to happiness. The gnomes corner him. They offer him a bottle. He resists for exactly two seconds and then he drinks it. The king turns orange. The king starts dancing. The kingdom of sadness has fallen. All hail the dairy empire.
label: king_falls

text: The film ends with everyone dancing together under a giant rainbow. The gnomes and the goblins, now all the same cheerful orange color, celebrating their unity through the power of processed milk. The music swells. The sun shines. And the message is absolutely clear: if someone is sad, do not talk to them. Do not understand them. Launch milk at them until they smile.
label: finale

text: I need to talk about the real story behind this cartoon because Borden Milk commissioned this during the Great Depression, and the context makes it even stranger. People were struggling, jobs were scarce, the world was bleak... and Borden's marketing strategy was essentially, what if a cartoon told everyone that milk fixes depression? Not therapy. Not community support. Milk. Specifically our milk. In bottles. Fired from catapults.
label: context

text: The Sunshine Makers was part of a whole series of industrial films that Borden produced. They had this thing called the Borden Company's advertising department that just churned out weird cartoons. There is another one about Elsie the Cow that I am not even going to get into because we would be here all night. But the throughline is always the same: milk solves everything. Every problem in human existence can be addressed with dairy.
label: borden_history

text: Alright chat, we have made it through the entire Sunshine Makers. Seven minutes and forty three seconds of the most beautiful, unhinged advertisement for milk that has ever existed. If you are just joining us, you missed gnomes launching catapults full of liquid sunshine at depressed goblins until they turned orange and danced. That is the plot. That is the entire film. And honestly? I think we all learned something tonight. Stay sunshine out there. Next cartoon coming up soon!
label: outro
